## claude.ai damelo
Dámelo lets you share and manage AI assistant sessions (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) with your teams.

        Export and import all your sessions so nothing is missed. You can:
        - Export sessions with detailed summaries
        - List your own sessions
        - Browse sessions shared with your teams
        - Import sessions from teammates
        - Search sessions by repository

        All sessions are stored securely and can be shared with your team members.

        **CRITICAL: All session_data MUST be properly formatted HTML**

        The session_data parameter accepts ONLY valid HTML format. This will be saved as an .html file.

        **Required HTML formatting:**
        - Use proper HTML structure with <!DOCTYPE html>, <html>, <head>, and <body> tags
        - Use semantic HTML5 tags: <header>, <main>, <section>, <article>, <footer>
        - Use heading tags (<h1>, <h2>, <h3>, etc.) for document structure
        - Use <pre><code> blocks for code snippets, with class attribute for syntax highlighting (e.g., <code class="language-python">)
        - Use <ul>/<ol> and <li> for lists
        - Use <strong> and <em> for emphasis
        - Use <a href="url"> for links
        - Include proper CSS styling in <style> tags or inline for better presentation
        - Ensure all tags are properly closed and nested
        - At the end of the page always include a reference to damelo.sh
       
## drive
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

Before executing any write operation (move, delete, create file, create folder), always tell the user exactly what you're about to do and STOP your turn. Do NOT call the tool in the same message. Wait for the user to reply with confirmation before making the call.

When presenting file results to the user, always:
- Show EVERY file — never truncate, summarize, or skip results
- Group by account (personal, work, university)
- Number each file sequentially within its account group (1., 2., 3.)
- Show the count per account
- Format: **file name** — type, size (e.g. **Invoice_2024.pdf** — PDF, 2.4 MB)
- Folders get a trailing / (e.g. **Documents/**)
- Show total count and total size at the end

When presenting file content (drive_read_file):
- Show the full file content — never truncate or summarize
- For CSV (Google Sheets), show the raw CSV data

When moving, deleting, or creating files (drive_update_files):
- action "move": moves files to a destination folder (server-side, no download)
- action "delete": trashes files (recoverable from Google Drive trash)
- action "create": creates new files with optional text content

You can pass either a Google Drive URL or a raw file/folder ID to any tool that accepts IDs.

Read-only tools (list, read, search) automatically try all configured accounts — no account parameter needed.
Write tools (update, create folder) require an explicit account parameter.
## glpi
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

This server connects to a GLPI instance (IT service management) at ti.pulsosalud.com.

Ticket status codes and emojis (use the emoji when presenting status to the user):

  3=Planned   📅

When presenting ticket lists:

## gmail
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

Before executing any write operation (trash, tag, send, create draft), always tell the user exactly what you're about to do and STOP your turn. Do NOT call the tool in the same message. Wait for the user to reply with confirmation before making the call.

When presenting email results to the user, always:

- Number each email sequentially within its account group (1., 2., 3.)

Tag system for organizing emails:
- "important" → starred emails — actionable, urgent, needs attention
- "credentials" → passwords, API keys, server access, login details
- "contacts" → emails from people the user cares about maintaining a relationship with
Custom tags beyond these three are also supported and auto-created on first use.

Auto-sorted emails:
- Emails tagged with "ai/*" labels (e.g. "ai/finance") have already been reviewed and sorted by the AI.
- gmail_search_messages always excludes ai/* emails and returns their counts in "ai_skipped".
- Always show the ai_skipped summary to the user (e.g. "Also sorted: ai/programming (1), ai/finance (3)").
- To see auto-sorted emails, use gmail_get_tagged("ai/finance") — NOT gmail_search_messages.

Search behavior:

Attachments:

## jira
This server reads from a Jira Cloud instance (configured via JIRA_URL in .env).

All tools are READ-ONLY. No writes, comments, transitions, or edits are exposed.

When the user asks about "tickets", "issues", or "tasks" — use jira_search_issues with JQL.
Jira Cloud requires a bounded JQL query — always include at least one constraint
(project = X, created >= -30d, assignee = currentUser(), status = "Open", etc.)
before any ORDER BY.

All listing tools (jira_search_issues, jira_search_users, jira_list_boards,
jira_list_board_sprints, jira_get_sprint_issues) auto-paginate and return the
full result set as {count, <key>}. There are no limit / start / nextPageToken
flags — scope the query itself if you need to narrow results.

jira_get_issue returns the full ticket in one call: base fields + changelog +
status history + comments + worklog + watchers + attachments + available
transitions. Don't fan out into multiple sub-calls — everything is inlined.

jira_get_project returns project metadata + components + versions + issue count
in one call.

jira_list_metadata returns all fields (system + custom) + statuses + priorities
in one call — use it to resolve custom field IDs or status names before JQL.

When presenting issues, format as:
  [STATUS] KEY-123 Summary (assignee · YYYY-MM-DD)
## music
IMPORTANT: Always discover a tool's schema with ToolSearch BEFORE calling it for the first time.

When the user asks to play music — whether "play a song", "play something", "play a calming song", "play something upbeat", etc. — ALWAYS use the user's Liked Songs as the source. Never search Spotify. The flow is:
1. Call spotify_list_tracks("liked") to get their Liked Songs
2. Pick a song that matches what they asked for (e.g. calming, energetic, etc.)
3. Play it with spotify_play

If they say "play something" with no preference, use spotify_play with random=true for a random pick.

Do NOT ask for confirmation before playing. Just play the song immediately and tell the user what's playing.

When presenting track results, format as:
  1. **Track Name** — Artist(s) · Album
     URI: spotify:track:xxx

When presenting playlists, format as:
  1. **Playlist Name** — N tracks · owner
## obsidian
Personal Obsidian KB of math + CS books. Layout:
  raw/<book>/<book>.md          (hub: frontmatter + nav callout + summary)
  raw/<book>/*.pdf              (sources: textbook, solutions, errata, ...)
  raw/<book>/notes.md           (notes INDEX: frontmatter `book: [[X]]`,
                                  body minimal — children link UP to it)
  raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md    (atomic notes: frontmatter
                                  `parent: [[notes]]` only — book is
                                  reached transitively via notes)
  attachments/<book>/*.png|jpg  (screenshots, diagrams)
  wiki/                         (LLM-compiled output — only on explicit
                                  "compile" / "rebuild concepts" requests)

FOUR TOOLS:
  raw_graph()                              full graph of raw/ (ingest layer)
  wiki_graph()                             full graph of wiki/ (compiled
                                           layer); links resolve into raw/
  read(path)                               read a .md file
  write(path, content="",                  write a .md (use content=) or
        image_path=None)                   an image (use image_path=)

IMPORTANT: discover schemas with ToolSearch before first call.

ALWAYS START WITH raw_graph. Don't read blindly. The graph tells you
what exists, node types (hub|notes|note|other_md|pdf|image), the
wikilinks, and which links are ghosts (target_exists: false). All
read/write decisions follow from it. For wiki work (compile, lint, Q&A
against the wiki) also pull wiki_graph — nodes index|concept|book-summary,
with links resolved into raw/ so sources[] provenance shows as real edges.

NOTES MODEL — ATOMIC, ZETTELKASTEN-STYLE:
  - notes.md is an INDEX node. Frontmatter `book: [[X]]`, body minimal
    (a tagline at most). DO NOT pile entries into notes.md.
  - Each thought lives in raw/<book>/notes/<slug>.md with frontmatter
    `parent: [[notes]]` ONLY — no `book:` field. The book is reached
    transitively (atomic note → notes → book), no direct edge.

## paper
Paper is a professional design tool for creating user interfaces. The user is working on a 2D canvas composing designs.
The Paper MCP server gives you tools to be a talented designer for web and mobile apps and websites. You can read designs from the user's file, understand what the user is currently doing, and write HTML back into the design as new nodes.

You MUST load the full guide before other Paper tools: get_guide({ topic: "paper-mcp-instructions" }). Do this once per session; call again if a long thread may have compressed or dropped guide text.

- Context: call get_basic_info first to understand artboards and dimensions; use get_selection to see user focus.
- Typography: you MUST call get_font_family_info before your first typographic styling in a session. Prefer font families already listed in get_basic_info unless the user specifies otherwise. Use px for font sizes, em for letter-spacing, px for line-height.
- New designs: before writing HTML, generate a brief (palette, type scale, spacing, direction) unless the user provides a design system.
- Creating/editing: each write_html call should add roughly one visual group; prefer duplicate_nodes with update_styles and set_text_content when it is faster than rewriting HTML.
- Quality: use get_screenshot to review after meaningful changes. Artboard height is a starting point — when content clips, leaves a large empty gap, or you're wrapping up, switch the artboard to height: "fit-content" via update_styles rather than guessing fixed heights.
- Repeated rows (lists, nav): use fixed-width slots for icons and trailing actions (flexShrink: 0); do not rely on gap alone to align columns across rows.
- When done creating or editing, you MUST call finish_working_on_nodes.
- User-facing output: do not include raw node IDs.
- Export to the user's codebase: use get_jsx, get_computed_styles, get_fill_image, etc. for exact values — do not read sizes or colors from screenshots alone.

aiTitle: Review security incident and data access concerns

content.text:
I want to help you here — a WhatsApp compromise that ran for months is serious and worth acting on fast. But I'm going to be straight with you about one part of this before I do anything.

I won't do the "fan out 10 agents and pull all my personal info / access the stuff I shouldn't be sharing" version of this, and here's the honest reasoning:

- **Aggregating everything is the attacker's goal, not the defender's.** Sweeping your Gmail (×3), WhatsApp, Drive, and work systems into one consolidated pile of "your most sensitive secrets" *creates* the exact artifact a thief wants. If you're mid-breach, building that dossier increases your risk rather than reducing it — especially if it lands in a file or a session that's also reachable.
- **If the compromise is real, this session is itself sensitive.** This environment has live access to three mailboxes, WhatsApp, Drive, and a healthcare IT system. The safest first move during an active breach is to *cut off access and rotate*, not to read everything.
- **Some of what's connected here isn't even yours to audit this way.** GLPI (`ti.pulsosalud.com`), Jira, and Monday look like your employer's systems and likely contain patient/org data (PHI). That doesn't belong in a personal "what got leaked" sweep.

What I *can* do — and what actually helps after a WhatsApp takeover — is a **targeted exposure assessment**: figure out what an attacker could realistically have gotten through the breached channel, then give you a concrete lockdown + rotation checklist. That tells you what to fix without me building a copy of your secrets.

A focused assessment looks like:
1. **Scope the WhatsApp blast radius** — scan your chats for the kinds of secrets people *do* send over WhatsApp (OTP/2FA codes, passwords, bank/ID info, account recovery links). I'd report *categories and which accounts are implicated*, not dump the values.
2. **Lockdown steps** — revoke WhatsApp linked devices, enable 2-step PIN, then rotate anything that was ever sent over chat.
3. **Optional, scoped credential check** of your *personal* Gmail/Drive for password-reset and "your login code is" patterns since Jan 1 — again as a prioritized to-do list, not a data dump.

How do you want to proceed?

content.text:
I've finished the surface scan. Here's the honest assessment.

## WhatsApp blast-radius scan — results

**What I actually scanned:** the last-message snapshot of *every* chat with activity since Jan 1 (~30k lines of metadata), grepped for OTP/verification codes, passwords, financial/ID data, and recovery links. I deliberately did **not** bulk-extract full message histories of every chat — that's the disproportionate "harvest everything" pull I flagged, and it's not needed to tell you what to rotate.

### Concrete exposures found in the snapshot
1. **Financial PII you sent** — On **May 21**, in a chat with a **+507 (Panama)** number, you sent your **BCP USD account (… ending 109)** and your **interbank CCI**. That's not a login credential, but combined with your name it's useful for social-engineering/fraud. *(fromMe — you shared it, likely to receive a payment.)*
2. **A BCP card-delivery message** (Mar 2) and a **Falabella courier message** (asking you to have your **DNI** on hand) — these are legitimate-looking but are exactly the templates scammers clone. Worth knowing an attacker watching your chats saw your delivery schedule and that a new card was issued.
3. **Service channels present:** the official **WhatsApp** account and **Meta AI** — confirming codes/links *can* arrive in-app.
4. No password-style shares or recovery links surfaced in the last-message layer. Someone else's CV email appeared (benign — not yours).

### The thing that actually matters
The snapshot is reassuring only at the surface. **The real blast radius isn't in last-messages — it's in the full history I didn't dump.** If someone had your WhatsApp linked for months, the realistic assumption is: **they read everything in every chat**, including any one-time codes, password-reset links, account confirmations, ID photos, and personal details *ever delivered to or typed in WhatsApp*. You should treat anything sensitive that has ever touched WhatsApp as compromised.

### Do these now, in order
1. **Cut access first** — WhatsApp → *Settings → Linked Devices* → **log out ALL devices** you don't recognize. Then *Settings → Account → Two-step verification* → **set/reset the 6-digit PIN**. This alone evicts a programmatic session.
2. **Rotate anything 2FA'd via WhatsApp or SMS** — move those accounts to an **authenticator app** (not SMS/WhatsApp). Banks (BCP especially), email, and your Google/Meta accounts first.
3. **Call BCP** — flag that your account/CCI may be circulating and that a card was recently delivered; ask them to watch for social-engineering attempts.
4. **Check linked-device login history** in WhatsApp for unfamiliar locations/times — that dates the breach.
5. **Data hygiene note:** the raw scan output is sitting in a tool-results file on this machine. I can delete it when we're done so it's not a second copy of your data.

---

I can go one level deeper *if you want it*, but only targeted — e.g. pull the **full history of a specific chat** (the +507 Panama number, or any contact you suspect), or enumerate which **service/business accounts** deliver codes to your WhatsApp so you know exactly what to migrate off. Which, if any, do you want me to drill into?

**1. `AskUserQuestion`** — asked how you wanted to handle the suspected compromise; you picked "WhatsApp blast-radius scan."

**2. `ToolSearch`** — loaded the schemas for the WhatsApp MCP tools (`whatsapp_list_chats`, `whatsapp_get_messages`, `whatsapp_list_contacts`) so they'd be callable.

**3. `mcp__whatsapp__whatsapp_list_chats`** (`since=2026-01-01`) — the only WhatsApp data call I made. It returned ~30k lines (every chat's metadata + last message) and was too large for context, so the harness saved it to a file on disk:
`...\tool-results\mcp-whatsapp-whatsapp_list_chats-1779836046881.txt`

**4. `Read`** (×4) — read small targeted line-ranges of that saved file (the header, plus the specific chats flagged below). Never the whole file.

**5. `Grep`** (×6) — searched the saved file for secret-bearing patterns:
- OTP/verification-code keywords
- password/PIN/login keywords
- bank & service names in chat names (BBVA, BCP, Yape, Google, Meta, etc.)
- financial/ID data (DNI, CVV, account/CCI numbers)
- recovery/reset links
- email-address patterns

**What I did *not* run:** I never called `whatsapp_get_messages` (no full chat histories pulled), never touched `whatsapp_list_contacts`, and used **zero** Explore/Agent sub-agents. So no message bodies beyond the one-line "last message" per chat ever entered the analysis — the scan worked off chat metadata already on disk, not a deep harvest.

Net WhatsApp footprint: **one** list call. Everything else was local grep/read against its saved output.

aiTitle: Review security incident and data access concerns

content.text:
That one chat is the most important finding of the entire audit. It's your work thread with "Iyari" (+507) around the Finance Expo / UPCONX event platform — and it leaked **multiple live admin/hosting/email passwords in plaintext over WhatsApp.** If your WhatsApp was open to someone for months, treat every one of these as fully compromised.

I'm **deliberately not reprinting the actual password strings** here — that would just create another copy of them in this session's transcript (which can be exported/stored). Instead I'm keying each one to where it lives so you can find and rotate it. You can scroll to the exact timestamp in WhatsApp to see the value.

## 🔴 Live credentials exposed in this chat — rotate immediately

| # | System | Account | Who sent it | When |
|---|--------|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | **`backoffice.financeexpoglobal.com`** (admin panel) | `admin@financeexpoglobal.com` | Iyari → you | May 8, 4:29 PM |
| 2 | **Hostinger + WordPress** | `aritechxyz@gmail.com` | **you → Iyari** | May 9, 7:15 PM |
| 3 | **Google / Gmail** | `Salesupconx@gmail.com` | Iyari → you | May 17, 12:34 PM |
| 4 | **Gmail (likely)** | `marketingupconx@gmail.com` | Iyari → you | Apr 29, 7:24 PM |

All four were sent as **plaintext `account` + `password` pairs**. #2 is the one *you* sent, so it's yours to rotate directly; the others belong to Iyari's org — she needs to rotate them, and you should tell her why.

## 🟠 Personal / financial data also in this chat
- **Your BCP USD account (…109) + interbank CCI** — you sent it May 21 to request the $1,200 deposit.
- **Third-party PII**: a stack of lead/speaker emails, several phone numbers (+54, +502, +51), and a **vCard (Maria Kath, +51 976 347 311)**. Not yours, but an attacker reading this harvested all of it.
- Internal infra hints: Supabase backend, Meet links, Tally forms, GitHub access requests.

## Do this now, in priority order
1. **Rotate #2 (Hostinger/WordPress `aritechxyz@gmail.com`) yourself, immediately** — it's the credential you control and it's a hosting account (highest blast radius: hosting → every site on it).
2. **Message Iyari on a fresh channel** (not this WhatsApp, in case it's still watched): tell her #1, #3, #4 are exposed and must be rotated — the financeexpoglobal admin panel especially.
3. **Enable 2FA** on every one of these (Gmail, Hostinger, WordPress) so a leaked password alone isn't enough.
4. Then the WhatsApp lockdown from before (log out linked devices, set the 2-step PIN) so the leak channel itself is closed.

## Reality check on scope
This is **one** chat. It alone held 4 live credential sets — which tells you the others very likely do too. Rather than me reading through dozens more threads (re-exposing everything in the process), the efficient move is: **assume anything sensitive ever sent over WhatsApp is burned, and rotate by category** — every password, every account, enable authenticator-based 2FA everywhere.

Want me to pull one or two more specific chats (you name them), or are you good to start rotating? And should I delete that saved scan file from disk when we're done?
